cb_step_1.title: Select CT Volume
cb_step_1.simple: Choose the CT scan of the fractured long bone from the list.
cb_step_1.detailed: This volume will be used for all subsequent segmentation and 3D reconstruction steps. Select the CT scan that clearly shows both the proximal (reference) and distal (moving) fracture fragments.

cb_step_2.title: Create Reference Segmentation
cb_step_2.simple: Switch to the Segment Editor module and create a new segmentation named 'Reference_Segmentation'.
cb_step_2.detailed: This segmentation will hold the bone fragment that will serve as the stable reference (typically the proximal fragment). Accurate segmentation is critical for successful fracture reduction planning.

cb_step_3.title: Add Reference Segment
cb_step_3.simple: Click the 'Add' button and rename the new segment to 'Reference_Segment'.
cb_step_3.detailed: This segment will contain the voxels belonging to the reference bone fragment. Naming it clearly helps avoid confusion when the moving fragment is created later.

cb_step_4.title: Open Threshold Tool
cb_step_4.simple: Click the 'Threshold' button in the Segment Editor.
cb_step_4.detailed: Thresholding isolates bone tissue from soft tissue by Hounsfield unit range. This prepares for automated segmentation of the reference fragment.

cb_step_5.title: Set Threshold Range
cb_step_5.simple: Adjust the threshold slider to include only bone voxels on the reference fragment.
cb_step_5.detailed: Set the lower and upper bounds to capture cortical and trabecular bone while excluding surrounding soft tissue and air. The range typically spans 150–3000 HU for adult bone.

cb_step_6.title: Apply Threshold
cb_step_6.simple: Click the 'Apply' button to create the threshold-based segmentation.
cb_step_6.detailed: This generates a binary mask of all bone voxels within the defined range. The mask will include both fragments and other bones, which will be cleaned up in the next steps.

cb_step_7.title: Open Islands Tool
cb_step_7.simple: Click the 'Islands' button in the Segment Editor.
cb_step_7.detailed: The Islands tool separates disconnected bone regions into individual islands, allowing you to select only the reference fragment and discard the rest.

cb_step_8.title: Select Keep Selected Island
cb_step_8.simple: Choose the 'Keep selected island' option from the Islands menu.
cb_step_8.detailed: This tool keeps only the island you click on and removes all other disconnected bone regions, isolating the intended reference fragment.

cb_step_9.title: Click Reference Fragment
cb_step_9.simple: Click on the reference bone fragment in a slice view to keep that island.
cb_step_9.detailed: This tells the tool which island to retain — typically the proximal fragment. All other bone (including the moving fragment) will be discarded from this segmentation.

cb_step_10.title: Hide Reference Segment
cb_step_10.simple: Toggle visibility of 'Reference_Segment' off so it does not interfere with the next steps.
cb_step_10.detailed: Hiding the completed reference segment keeps the view clean while you create the segmentation for the moving fragment.

cb_step_11.title: Create Moving Segmentation
cb_step_11.simple: Create a new segmentation named 'Moving_Segmentation' in the Segment Editor.
cb_step_11.detailed: This segmentation will hold the other bone fragment (typically the distal fragment). It is moved relative to the reference during fracture reduction.

cb_step_12.title: Add Moving Segment
cb_step_12.simple: Click 'Add' and rename the new segment to 'Moving_Segment'.
cb_step_12.detailed: This segment will contain the bone voxels of the moving fragment. Clear naming prevents confusion with the already-completed reference segment.

cb_step_13.title: Open Threshold Tool
cb_step_13.simple: Click the 'Threshold' button again for the moving segmentation.
cb_step_13.detailed: Repeat the threshold operation to isolate the moving fragment's bone tissue within its own segmentation.

cb_step_14.title: Set Moving Threshold Range
cb_step_14.simple: Adjust the threshold slider to capture bone voxels of the moving fragment.
cb_step_14.detailed: Use a bone-appropriate threshold range (similar to step 5) to capture the moving fragment while excluding soft tissue.

cb_step_15.title: Apply Threshold
cb_step_15.simple: Click 'Apply' to create the threshold mask for the moving segment.
cb_step_15.detailed: This creates a binary mask that may include both fragments again; the next steps will isolate the moving fragment using the Islands tool.

cb_step_16.title: Open Islands Tool
cb_step_16.detailed: Prepare to isolate the moving fragment by selecting the appropriate island.

cb_step_17.title: Select Keep Selected Island
cb_step_17.simple: Choose 'Keep selected island' from the Islands menu.
cb_step_17.detailed: This will keep only the island you click next, isolating the moving fragment from any other bone in the mask.

cb_step_18.title: Click Moving Fragment
cb_step_18.simple: Click on the moving bone fragment in a slice view to keep that island.
cb_step_18.detailed: This selects the distal fragment as the moving fragment. The reference fragment and other bones will be removed from this segmentation.

cb_step_19.title: Hide Moving Segment
cb_step_19.detailed: Hiding the completed moving segment keeps the view uncluttered before proceeding to 3D reconstruction.

cb_step_20.title: Select Reference Segmentation
cb_step_20.simple: In the extension panel, choose 'Reference_Segmentation' as the reference segmentation.
cb_step_20.detailed: This tells the extension which segmentation contains the reference fragment for 3D model creation and fracture analysis.

cb_step_21.title: Reconstruct Reference Model
cb_step_21.detailed: This generates a 3D surface model of the reference bone fragment, which will be used for fracture surface detection and alignment.

cb_step_22.title: Select Moving Segmentation
cb_step_22.detailed: This designates the segmentation containing the fragment that will be repositioned to achieve anatomical reduction.

cb_step_23.title: Reconstruct Moving Model
cb_step_23.detailed: This creates a 3D model of the moving fragment. Both 3D models are needed for fracture surface detection and virtual reduction.

cb_step_24.title: Add Reference ROI
cb_step_24.simple: Click the 'Add / reset reference ROI' button to create a region of interest around the reference fragment.
cb_step_24.detailed: The ROI defines the volume used for fracture surface detection on the reference fragment. It should tightly enclose the fracture end.

cb_step_25.title: Adjust Reference ROI
cb_step_25.simple: Drag the ROI handles to enclose the fractured portion of the reference model.
cb_step_25.detailed: Narrow the ROI so it only covers the fracture site — not the entire bone. This focuses the fracture detection algorithm on the matching surfaces that need to align during reduction.

cb_step_26.title: Add Moving ROI
cb_step_26.simple: Click the 'Add / reset moving ROI' button to create a region of interest around the moving fragment.
cb_step_26.detailed: This ROI will be used to detect the corresponding fracture surface on the moving fragment.

cb_step_27.title: Adjust Moving ROI
cb_step_27.simple: Drag the ROI handles to enclose the fractured portion of the moving model.
cb_step_27.detailed: Tightly enclose the fracture end of the moving fragment. The fracture detection algorithm will search for matching surface geometry within both ROIs to compute the optimal reduction.

cb_step_28.title: Detect Fracture Surfaces
cb_step_28.detailed: The algorithm identifies the opposing fracture surfaces within the defined ROIs. These surfaces will be used as targets for aligning the fragments.

cb_step_29.title: Reduce Fracture
cb_step_29.simple: Click the 'Reduce fracture' button to align the moving fragment to the reference fragment.
cb_step_29.detailed: This computes the rigid transformation that best fits the fracture surfaces together, virtually reducing the fracture. The result is a transformation that can be applied to the moving fragment.

cb_step_30.title: Manual Adjustment Option
cb_step_30.simple: Check the box if you want to manually adjust the moving fragment position after automatic reduction.
cb_step_30.detailed: If the automatic result looks good, skip this step. Otherwise, checking the box enables manual fine-tuning of the fragment alignment using the 3D view.

cb_step_31.title: Inspect and Adjust View
cb_step_31.simple: Rotate and zoom the 3D view to inspect the alignment of the moving fragment.
cb_step_31.detailed: Carefully examine the gap and rotation between fragments from multiple angles. A clear vantage helps you decide if further adjustment with the transform tools is needed.

cb_step_32.title: Harden Transform
cb_step_32.simple: Click the 'Harden transform into moving fragment' button to finalize the reduction.
cb_step_32.detailed: This permanently applies the transformation to the moving fragment's geometry, making the reduction a fixed part of the model for subsequent planning or surgical guide creation.
